annotations.label: Aleph-zero

anchor.snippet: is the cardinality of the set of all natural numbers

annotations.note: Defined as `Cardinal.aleph0 := lift #ℕ`, with `Cardinal.mk_nat : #ℕ = ℵ₀` giving exactly the Wikipedia definition.

annotations.label: Characterization of cardinality aleph-zero

anchor.snippet: A set has cardinality

annotations.note: `Cardinal.mk_eq_aleph0 : [Countable α] [Infinite α] → #α = ℵ₀` characterizes cardinality ℵ₀ as countably infinite (denumerable).

annotations.label: Well-ordering of positive integers with order type ω+ω

anchor.snippet: the sequence (with order type

annotations.note: Grep for an explicit ω+ω order on the positive integers yields no specific construction in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Aleph-zero is the smallest infinite cardinal under countable choice

anchor.snippet: If the axiom of countable choice

annotations.note: `aleph0_le_mk_iff : ℵ₀ ≤ #α ↔ Infinite α` proves the conclusion in Mathlib's choice-based foundation (stronger than the countable-choice hypothesis Wikipedia mentions).

annotations.label: Aleph-one

anchor.snippet: is the cardinality of the set of all countable ordinal numbers

annotations.note: Notation `ℵ₁ := aleph 1` together with `succ_aleph0 : succ ℵ₀ = ℵ₁` and `ord_aleph 1 = ω₁` show ℵ₁ is the cardinality of countable ordinals.

annotations.label: No cardinal strictly between aleph-zero and aleph-one

anchor.snippet: implies (in ZF, Zermelo–Fraenkel set theory without the axiom of choice) that no cardinal number is between

annotations.note: `aleph_one_le_iff : ℵ₁ ≤ c ↔ ℵ₀ < c` (equivalently `lt_aleph_one_iff`) states that nothing lies strictly between ℵ₀ and ℵ₁.

annotations.label: Countable subsets of omega-one have an upper bound

anchor.snippet: Any countable subset of

annotations.note: `isRegular_aleph_one` together with `bsup_lt_ord_of_isRegular` gives boundedness of small-cardinality families in ω₁, but no single lemma is stated directly as `Countable s → BddAbove s` for `s ⊆ ω₁`.

annotations.label: Closing under countable-arity operations using omega-one

anchor.snippet: An example application of the ordinal

annotations.note: No general closure-under-countable-operations construction using ω₁ appears as a stated lemma in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cardinality of the continuum

anchor.snippet: The cardinality of the set of real numbers

annotations.note: `Cardinal.continuum := 2 ^ ℵ₀` (notation `𝔠`), and `Cardinal.mk_real : #ℝ = 𝔠` in `Mathlib.Analysis.Real.Cardinality`.

annotations.label: Continuum hypothesis

anchor.snippet: The CH states that there is no set whose cardinality is strictly between that of the natural numbers and the real numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib proves only `aleph_one_le_continuum : ℵ₁ ≤ 𝔠`; the CH equality `ℵ₁ = 𝔠` is independent of ZFC and not stated.

annotations.label: Independence of CH from ZFC

anchor.snippet: CH is independent of ZFC

annotations.note: Mathlib has no metamathematical formalization of forcing/models giving the independence of CH.

annotations.label: Aleph-omega

anchor.snippet: Aleph-omega is

annotations.note: `ℵ_ ω` instantiates `Cardinal.aleph` at the first infinite ordinal; e.g. `isSingular_aleph_omega0 : (ℵ_ ω).IsSingular` uses exactly this value.

annotations.label: Aleph-omega not equal to continuum

anchor.snippet: is the first uncountable cardinal number that can be demonstrated within Zermelo–Fraenkel set theory not to be equal to the cardinality of the set of all real numbers

annotations.note: `isSingular_aleph_omega0` shows ℵ_ω has cofinality ω; combined with König's theorem (cof(2^ℵ₀) > ℵ₀) this gives ℵ_ω ≠ 𝔠, but no direct lemma `aleph_omega ≠ continuum` is stated.

annotations.label: Cofinality omega

anchor.snippet: An uncountably infinite cardinal

annotations.note: Cofinality is defined as `Ordinal.cof`, and `IsSingular`/`IsRegular` capture the cofinality = ω case (cf. `isSingular_aleph_iff`).

annotations.label: Successor cardinal operation

anchor.snippet: we must define the successor cardinal operation

annotations.note: `SuccOrder Cardinal` instance gives `Order.succ : Cardinal → Cardinal`, with `succ_def : succ c = sInf {c' | c < c'}`.

annotations.label: Aleph numbers for general ordinal

anchor.snippet: We can then define the aleph numbers as follows

annotations.note: `Cardinal.aleph : Ordinal ↪o Cardinal` defines `ℵ_ α` for any ordinal α; `succ_aleph` and `aleph_limit` recover the standard transfinite recursion.

annotations.label: Initial ordinal notation

anchor.snippet: -th infinite initial ordinal is written

annotations.note: `Ordinal.omega` (notation `ω_ o`) enumerates infinite initial ordinals; `Ordinal.IsInitial` defines initiality and `isInitial_omega` shows `ω_ o` is initial.

annotations.label: Index of an infinite cardinal via transfinite induction

anchor.snippet: Alternatively, for every infinite cardinal

annotations.note: `mem_range_aleph_iff : c ∈ range aleph ↔ ℵ₀ ≤ c` says every infinite cardinal is `ℵ_ α` for some ordinal α.

annotations.label: Aleph as a function-like class on ordinals

anchor.snippet: the aleph function

annotations.note: `Cardinal.aleph : Ordinal ↪o Cardinal` is the order-embedding form; `isNormal_aleph` shows it is a normal function.

annotations.label: Alpha bounded by aleph-alpha

anchor.snippet: For any ordinal

annotations.note: `le_omega_self : o ≤ omega o` (and the cardinal form `Ordinal.card_le_aleph : o.card ≤ ℵ_ o`) state α ≤ ω_α.

annotations.label: Strict inequality at successor ordinals

anchor.snippet: it is true for any successor ordinal

annotations.note: `aleph_lt_aleph` (and `succ_aleph`) imply `ℵ_ α < ℵ_ (α+1)`, giving strict inequality `α < ℵ_ α` at successors.

annotations.label: First fixed point of the aleph function

anchor.snippet: The first such is the limit of the sequence

annotations.note: Grep finds no construction of the first ℵ-fixed point (limit of ℵ₀, ℵ_{ℵ₀}, …) in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Weakly inaccessible cardinals are fixed points of aleph

anchor.snippet: Any weakly inaccessible cardinal is also a fixed point of the aleph function

annotations.note: Mathlib's `IsInaccessible` is strongly inaccessible; `IsInaccessible.aleph_ord : ℵ_ c.ord = c` gives the fixed-point property for the strong version, but weakly inaccessible cardinals are not defined.

annotations.label: Every aleph is the cardinality of some ordinal; initial ordinal

anchor.snippet: Every aleph is the cardinality of some ordinal

annotations.note: `ord_aleph : (ℵ_ o).ord = ω_ o` together with `isInitial_omega` shows every aleph is the cardinality of its initial ordinal.

annotations.label: Finite sets are well-orderable but not alephs

anchor.snippet: Each finite set is well-orderable

annotations.note: `range_aleph = Set.Ici ℵ₀` says the alephs are exactly the infinite cardinals, so finite cardinals are not alephs (well-orderability of finite types is automatic).

annotations.label: Every cardinality is an aleph iff axiom of choice

anchor.snippet: the assumption that the cardinality of each infinite set is an aleph number is equivalent to the existence of a well-ordering of every set

annotations.note: Mathlib works in ZFC where AC is built in, so it does not formalize the ZF equivalence `(every infinite cardinal is an aleph) ↔ AC`.

annotations.label: Scott's trick for cardinal representatives

anchor.snippet: The method of Scott's trick

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Cardinal` via quotients/equivalence of types (not Scott's trick), and grep finds no `Scott` construction for cardinal representatives.
